key_points: Food Security means availability, accessibility, and affordability of food to all people at all times.
Availability refers to food production within the country, food imports, and stocks stored in government granaries.
Accessibility means food is within reach of every person without any barrier.
Affordability implies that an individual has enough money to buy sufficient, safe, and nutritious food.
The poorest sections of society are the most food-insecure (e.g., landless laborers, traditional artisans, casual workers).
Hunger is not just a result of poverty; it has two dimensions: Chronic (long-term inadequate diet) and Seasonal (related to cycles of food growing).
To ensure food security, the Indian government designed the Food Security System consisting of two components: Buffer Stock and Public Distribution System (PDS).
Cooperatives and NGOs also play a vital role in providing food at controlled rates (e.g., Mother Dairy, Amul).
formulas: Buffer Stock = Foodgrains (Wheat/Rice) procured by FCI from surplus states.
Issue Price = Price at which grains are distributed to the poor (Lower than market price).
Minimum Support Price (MSP) = Pre-announced price paid to farmers for their crops.
examples: Famine: The 1943 Famine of Bengal which killed 30 lakh people.
Buffer Stock: The Food Corporation of India (FCI) buying wheat from a farmer in Punjab to store in a godown.
PDS (Ration Shops): A family using a BPL card to buy rice and sugar at a subsidized rate from a local fair price shop.
Cooperatives: Mother Dairy providing milk and vegetables at prices set by the government in Delhi.
Grain Banks: NGOs in Maharashtra helping tribal communities store grain for the lean season.
exam_tips: Memorize the 'Three A's' of Food Security: Availability, Accessibility, and Affordability.
Understand that MSP is for the 'Farmer' (incentive to grow) and Issue Price is for the 'Consumer' (to make it affordable).
Identify the role of the FCI (Food Corporation of India) as the agency that manages procurement and storage.
Differentiate between Chronic Hunger (poor quality/quantity always) and Seasonal Hunger (jobless months in farming).
Remember that the Green Revolution (late 1960s) helped India become self-sufficient in foodgrains.
